title: Apply: Reconstruction: An Overview
description: Apply your understanding of Reconstruction with this set of multiple-choice questions.
topic_path: America’s Second Founding: Slavery, the Civil War, Emancipation, and Reconstruction

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Civil Rights Act of 1866.**

Be it enacted . . . [t]hat all persons born in the United States and not subject to any foreign power, excluding Indians not taxed, are hereby declared to be citizens of the United States; and such citizens, of every race and color, without regard to any previous condition of slavery or involuntary servitude, except as a punishment for crime whereof the party shall have been duly convicted, shall have the same right, in every State and Territory in the United States, to make and enforce contracts, to sue, be parties, and give evidence, to inherit, purchase, lease, sell, hold, and convey real and personal property, and to full and equal benefit of all laws and proceedings for the security of person and property, as is enjoyed by white citizens, and shall be subject to like punishment, pains, and penalties, and to none other, any law, statute, ordinance, regulation, or custom, to the contrary notwithstanding.

**According to the excerpt, what was the purpose of the Civil Rights Act of 1866?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: To free enslaved people from bondage
To extend voting rights to formerly enslaved people
To guarantee citizenship to formerly enslaved people
To restore the rights of ex-Confederates

assessment_items.question_content:
|| **Number of African American Members in Congress (1869–1877)** ||
Congress | Members in the House | Members in the Senate | Total
- | - | - | -
41st (1869–1871) | 2 | 1  | 3
42nd (1871–1873) | 5 | 0 | 5
43rd (1873–1875) | 7 | 0 | 7
44th (1875–1877) | 7 | 1 | 8


**Based on the information in the table, choose the phrase that best completes the sentence.**

**The number of African American Members in Congress  \_\_\_\_ between 1865 and 1877.**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation: Congressional Research Service, African American Members of the U.S. Congress: 1870-2020, [CRSReports](https://crsreports.congress.gov/product/pdf/RL/RL30378), 2020.

choices.content: decreased significantly
decreased slightly
stayed the same
increased gradually

**Which piece of legislation most likely contributed to the trend in the table?**

[[☃ radio 2]]

choices.content: The Thirteenth Amendment
The Fifteenth Amendment
The Civil Rights Act of 1866
The Civil Rights Act of 1875

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

**Which piece of legislation in the post-Civil War period relates most closely to the subject of this political cartoon?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation: J. L. Magee, “Emancipation: ‘And by virtue of the power and for the purpose aforesaid, I do order and declare that all persons held as slaves . . . shall be free!,’” [Library of Congress](https://www.loc.gov/item/2020780949/), 1865.

options.alt: Lincoln stands in the center of the cartoon. He holds up his right hand, which is pointing at the words, "Freedom for All. Both Black and White!" In his left hand, he holds a document that reads "Proclamation of Emancipation." A group of Black and White Americans kneels at Lincoln's feet and looks up at him gratefully. There is a broken set of chains on the ground.

choices.content: The Thirteenth Amendment
The Black Codes
The Civil Rights Act of 1866
The Reconstruction Act of 1867

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

**What event is depicted in the political cartoon?**

[[☃ radio 2]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

options.explanation: J. L. Magee, “Emancipation: ‘And by virtue of the power and for the purpose aforesaid, I do order and declare that all persons held as slaves . . . shall be free!,’” [Library of Congress](https://www.loc.gov/item/2020780949/), 1865.

options.alt: Lincoln stands in the center of the cartoon. He holds up his right hand, which is pointing at the words, "Freedom for All. Both Black and White!" In his left hand, he holds a document that reads "Proclamation of Emancipation." A group of Black and White Americans kneels at Lincoln's feet and looks up at him gratefully. There is a broken set of chains on the ground.

choices.content: President Lincoln passing a law that outlawed slavery in the border states
Confederate supporters protesting against President Lincoln’s treatment of the South
President Lincoln issuing an order that freed enslaved people in the Confederate states
Abolitionists trying to persuade Lincoln that the Emancipation Proclamation didn’t go far enough

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Reconstruction Acts of 1867.**

And be it further enacted, That when the people of any one of said rebel States shall have formed a constitution of government in [[☃ definition 1]] with the Constitution of the United States in all respects, framed by a convention of delegates elected by the male citizens of said State, twenty-one years old and upward, of whatever race, color, or previous condition,...when such constitution shall provide that the [[☃ definition 2]] shall be enjoyed by all such persons as have the qualifications herein stated for electors of delegates, and when such constitution shall be ratified by a majority of the persons voting on the question of ratification who are qualified as electors for delegates, and when such constitution shall have been submitted to Congress for examination and approval, and Congress shall have approved the same, and when said State, by a vote of its legislature elected under said constitution, shall have adopted the amendment to the Constitution of the United States, proposed by the Thirty-ninth Congress, and known as article fourteen, and when such article shall have become a part of the Constitution of the United States, said State shall be declared entitled to representation in Congress and senators and representatives shall be admitted therefrom on their taking the oath prescribed by law, and then and thereafter the preceding sections of this act shall be inoperative in said State...

**What was the primary goal of the Reconstruction Acts, as described in this passage?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: To explain the process for readmitting southern states to the Union
To formally end the practice of slavery throughout the United States
To negotiate the terms of surrender for the former Confederate states
To prevent race-based discrimination in public places in the South

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Reconstruction Acts of 1867.**

And be it further enacted, That when the people of any one of said rebel States shall have formed a constitution of government in [[☃ definition 1]] with the Constitution of the United States in all respects, framed by a convention of delegates elected by the male citizens of said State, twenty-one years old and upward, of whatever race, color, or previous condition,...when such constitution shall provide that the [[☃ definition 2]] shall be enjoyed by all such persons as have the qualifications herein stated for electors of delegates, and when such constitution shall be ratified by a majority of the persons voting on the question of ratification who are qualified as electors for delegates, and when such constitution shall have been submitted to Congress for examination and approval, and Congress shall have approved the same, and when said State, by a vote of its legislature elected under said constitution, shall have adopted the amendment to the Constitution of the United States, proposed by the Thirty-ninth Congress, and known as article fourteen, and when such article shall have become a part of the Constitution of the United States, said State shall be declared entitled to representation in Congress and senators and representatives shall be admitted therefrom on their taking the oath prescribed by law, and then and thereafter the preceding sections of this act shall be inoperative in said State...

**According to this passage, what is one requirement that the Southern states had to meet to be readmitted to the Union?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: To promise never to secede again
To give African Americans the right to vote
To provide jobs for formerly enslaved people
To vote for Andrew Johnson for president

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

**Which statement best describes the message of the political cartoon?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.explanation: “Mending the Family Kettle,” *Frank Leslie’s Illustrated Newspaper,* [Dickinson College](https://hd.housedivided.dickinson.edu/node/45425), 1866.

options.alt: In the image, President Andrew Johnson holds a leaking kettle labeled “The Reconstructed South.” He faces a woman who represents Columbia, a common symbol of the United States and liberty. She wears a cap labeled “liberty.” Columbia holds a baby wearing clothing that says “constitutional amendment.”. Columbia says, “Now, Andy, I wish you and your boys would hurry up that job, because I want to use that kettle right away. You are all talking too much about it.”
options.caption: “Mending the Family Kettle,” 1866. The woman on the left is Columbia, a symbol of the United States.

choices.content: The president had to get congressional approval for the military occupation of the former Confederate states.
The United States refused to accept the Southern states back into the Union until the president accepted the surrender of the Southern states.
The president’s Reconstruction efforts did not address ongoing issues and instability, keeping the southern states from returning to the Union.
The Union agreed to the president’s Reconstruction plan for the South and would accept the former Confederate states back into the Union.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Richard Henry Dana Jr.’s 1865 “Grasp of War” speech, which commented on President Andrew Johnson’s Reconstruction plans for North Carolina and Mississippi. Dana had served as a U.S. Attorney during the Civil War.**

The slaves are emancipated. In form, this is true. But the public faith stands pledged to them, that they and their [[☃ definition 1]] forever shall have a complete and perfect freedom. . . . Then, how shall we secure to them a complete and perfect freedom? The constitution of every slave state is cemented in slavery. Their statute-books are full of slavery. It is the corner-stone of every rebel state. If you allow them to come back at once, without condition, into the exercise of all their state functions, what [[☃ definition 2]] have you for the complete freedom of the men you emancipate? There must, therefore, not merely be an emancipation of the actual, living slaves, but there must be an abolition of the slave system.

**Which statement best summarizes Dana’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Emancipation Proclamation has ended the institution of slavery and ensured that no person will be able to own slaves.
Although it’s true that slavery has been abolished, Black Americans will not have total freedom until they have the right to vote.
The former slave states should be readmitted to the United States as Black Americans are no longer enslaved.
The Confederate states cannot rejoin the Union until they eliminate the laws that uphold the slave system.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Richard Henry Dana Jr.’s 1865 “Grasp of War” speech, which commented on President Andrew Johnson’s Reconstruction plans for North Carolina and Mississippi. Dana had served as a U.S. Attorney during the Civil War.**

The slaves are emancipated. In form, this is true. But the public faith stands pledged to them, that they and their [[☃ definition 1]] forever shall have a complete and perfect freedom. . . . Then, how shall we secure to them a complete and perfect freedom? The constitution of every slave state is cemented in slavery. Their statute-books are full of slavery. It is the corner-stone of every rebel state. If you allow them to come back at once, without condition, into the exercise of all their state functions, what [[☃ definition 2]] have you for the complete freedom of the men you emancipate? There must, therefore, not merely be an emancipation of the actual, living slaves, but there must be an abolition of the slave system.

**Which legislative measure solved the problem Dana describes in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Thirteenth Amendment
The Civil Rights Act of 1866
The Fourteenth Amendment
General Sherman’s Field Order 15

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

**Which statement best describes the message of the political cartoon?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.explanation: Nast, Thomas, “Awkward Collision on the Grand Trunk Columbia” [National Park Service](https://www.nps.gov/anjo/images/Reconstruction-Leslie-s-1866-2.jpg), 1866.

options.alt:
Two steam engines are heading for collision on a single track.

A.J. (Driver of Engine "President" and flying a flag that states “36 not 25 states”) - "Look here! One of us has got to back!"
Thaddeus (Driver of Engine "Congress" and flying a flag that states “No Surrender”) - "Well it ain't me that's going to do it! You bet!"
options.caption: “Awkward Collision on the Grand Trunk Columbia” by Thomas Nast, 1866.

choices.content: The president and Congress refused to allow southern states back into the Union until they ratified the Fourteenth Amendment.
The president and Congress often clashed over their approaches to Reconstruction.
The president and Congress refused to compromise over the expansion of slavery across the United States.
The president and Congress came into conflict over presidential war powers during the Civil War.

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ explanation 1]]

**This political cartoon best illustrates which development in the years after the Civil War?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.explanation: Nast, Thomas, “Awkward Collision on the Grand Trunk Columbia” [National Park Service](https://www.nps.gov/anjo/images/Reconstruction-Leslie-s-1866-2.jpg), 1866.

options.alt:
Two steam engines are heading for collision on a single track.

A.J. (Driver of Engine "President" and flying a flag that states “36 not 25 states”) - "Look here! One of us has got to back!"
Thaddeus (Driver of Engine "Congress" and flying a flag that states “No Surrender”) - "Well it ain't me that's going to do it! You bet!"
options.caption: “Awkward Collision on the Grand Trunk Columbia” by Thomas Nast, 1866.

choices.content: The efforts of Congress to restrict the president’s wartime powers, including the power to deploy troops without congressional approval
The ideological differences within the Republican Party on giving Black Americans the right to vote
The legislative efforts by southern states to restrict the political and economic rights of formerly enslaved people
The political split between Andrew Johnson and Republicans in Congress about when and how to end Reconstruction
